author: @Afro408
text: Like you, I also thought these rods were a high Zinc alloy and when I first saw these demonstrated, the rep was punching a hole in the base of an empty drink can with a screwdriver, then sealing it back up again without melting the can with the propane torch! 😳 Of course when I tried, I used the normal brazing technique I’d been shown and heated the can first ( just like you did  Robert ) and melted the can. 😏🤔. He just said to me “Heat the rod before the job if it’s thin and rub the rod over ( or along ) the spot you want to join”. It seemed counterintuitive to me, but that’s the trick, as the rubbing removes the minute amount of Aluminium oxide on the surface of the metal. This method works well for me using these Zinc rods.

author: @goforgod8099
text:
These "easy aluminum welding rods" are generally 98% zinc, 2% alu
They generally have a flux inside, non corrosive (a complex of tiny particles of cesium-fluoroaluminate, along with polymer and metallic additives). it doesn't absorb moisture, no corrosion effect. The problem is the difficulty to buy such cesium-based flux; seems to be only commercialised for industries. So the only solution is corrosive flux at the moment (zinc chloride is easy to do).

And before brazing clean the parts and zinc with high grade alcohol (methanol, etc), then rub the alu with a stainless steel brush, then apply the flux and braze quickly.

author: @decyrano
text: Thermalloy is an alloy with an integrated flux, the major components of which are zinc and tin.The melting temp is around 500 degrees F. The product has been advertised as an aluminum solder, and been commercially available since the 1950's, from the company with the same name, Thermalloy. There are some interesting, unexpected uses for Thermalloy that border on the unbelievable, related to hydrogen production and gardening. You can occasionally find vintage packages on ebay, which are the best for doing those exotic experiments.

author: @jwioo
text: In an extract from a 1932 Popular Science, it states that aluminium can be soldered using a mixture of tin, zinc and aluminium, and that the higher content of zinc and aluminium the stiffer the mixture is at low temps, so requires higher working temps but that the joint is stronger. It goes on to say that if if joints are tinned with this mixture, the joint can subsequently be soldered with ordinary solder. Also that the because the solder is electro-positive compared to Al, is corrodes readily on contact with water so needs painting. I suspect the modern "Aliwelds" have other additives but maybe not.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: probably not mate - it's actually quite amazing how much stuff is from the 19th and early 20th century

author: @funkysod
text: Have used zinc to braze holes in aluminium radiators. It helps to scratch the surface of the base metal when the molten zinc sits on top. Makes it flow, I suspect it disturbs the oxide which melting temperature is higher than aluminium if I remember correctly. Later found out this sometimes was used in the vintage car community for fixing aluminium parts.

replies.author: @littleworkshopofhorrors2395
replies.text: Stainless steel rod is recommended, in fact stipulated as is the use of stainless brushes for a preclean. I wonder what the problem is with iron contamination, over to you Robert.

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: The way I understand it, the oxide layer prevents the molten bead from bonding - think of it like teflon.  But the oxide monolayer is only a few atoms thick and easily disturbed.  This layer would usually reform nearly as fast as it is breached if exposed to oxygen.  But if you're scratching at that layer beneath the molten bead, the bead keeps the oxygen out and then the bead can actually burrow underneath the layer some, making it flow nicely.

replies.author: @littleworkshopofhorrors2395
replies.text: @NorthernKitty that is my understanding too.

replies.author: @funkysod
replies.text: @NorthernKitty Yes, well explained!
